text:
Many of us sign up for a home loan EMI, when we start our first job.

We think it is better to pay an EMI of let’s say 25K, than wasting money by paying a 25K rent.

But, did you analyze the downside fully?

- EMI means that you are locked in for 10-15-20 years
- Your EMI might change with interest rates. Eg. instead of 10K, the payment can suddenly become 12K
- You might move cities, and might not live in your own house
- You might lose your job, and might stuck paying the 10K

You get the picture.

Most people start asking these questions (or experiencing the outcomes) once they get locked into an investment. And, eventually regret.

Like it or not: you're an investor.

You're making critical investment calls with your money, time all throughout your life.

The job you pick.
The side hustle you create.
Your critical decision to buy a house vs not.

Every decision helps you understand your downside/upside.
More decisions you make, the better you get.

The trick is: to make enough low-impact financial decisions (& learn) before you are forced to make some critical ones.

In our 20s, we deeply value money.
By our 30s, we also start valuing lifestyle/quality of life.

Peace, family time, free time starts mattering a lot more in our 30s than 20s.

This same framework applies all through our lives.

In our working years, we’d often say that – “where should I invest?” The goal here is to grow wealth. And, to grow it fast.

You can afford the risk. Why? Because a job gives us a cash flow. It acts as a cushion.

Our lifestyle is built around this income stream.
Investing for growth is just an outcome.

Compare this to someone who just retired.
The conversation changes to “how do I sustain my lifestyle?”

You did not change.
Your situation did.

Resultantly, so did your investing goals. And, investing style.

Point is: Best investors evolve with time.
They know what type of life they want.
They plan ahead. Manage their risks.

This requires active work.
Because your life circumstances requires active management.

People spend 8-10 hours working actively to build an income.
But, refrain from spending 2 hours/week managing it.

The ones who do: actually reap immense benefits.
Money management has a strong confluence with life management.

Last two years, my wealth skyrocketed. I built out a 1Mn$ portfolio for my students. And, I now sit at 50%+ gains in US$ terms. (Almost 65% in INR terms).

Most importantly 1000s of members of my community are now invested in the AI trade with me. And, have benefitted immensely.

The hardest challenge I had to overcome in this journey was: to not listen to "experts".

The simple rule I follow: if I can't see someone invest their own money, why trust them?

This is the same philosophy with which I have built my investment communities.

No one is an expert when it comes to markets.
And, I can very well be wrong about my investments.

The only thing I do for my community is: that I actually put money behind my bets. And, if I am wrong -- I will lose money too.

I always wanted to teach things practically.
And, investing money and going through the journey is as practical as it gets.

****

This weekend, I am doing a detailed session on Portfolio Building for my community. If you wish to be a part, check here: https://lnkd.in/d2Gq9KA7

A financial reset is coming.

Countries will ditch the US$, US will ditch the world trade, China will dump its goods on others.

(All valid possibilities)

What's coming is dangerous.
And, here is what you do:-

1) Buy some gold (physical) at every support. This is your safety engine.
2) Do NOT trust the government 1 bit. They can put retrospective taxation on digital version of gold/silver.
3) All people that you see buying "digital" gold via Mutual Funds are big time fools. And, will get screwed at some point.
4) Put a decent chunk of your money on "growth" assets. Things that improve productivity. Eg. Tech. Within tech AI. This is your growth engine.
5) Do NOT make large commitments. Eg. if you can afford a house worth 10Cr. Buy worth 2Cr.
6) Having investment options across multiple countries. Example: buy your US stocks directly; get access to South East Asia market etc. Yes, you need to be a HNI or whatever.
7) But, if you are working with small capital: pick gold, growth equities (primarily the US).
8) Do not trust derivative products like REITs. Another scam is brewing here (big time), especially in markets like India.
9) Builders + Politician lobby is deeply corrupt = REITs will give them more leverage. Be safe!
10) Keep 60-70% of your capital in liquid assets. Opportunities = crashes = great wealth!

Build a portfolio that survives.

I'm doing exactly that. And, helping my community do the same.

> China's richest person (Zhang Yiming): 70Bn$
> US's richest person (Elon): 850Bn$
> India's richest person: 100Bn$

Size of India's GDP = 4Tr$
Size of China's GDP = 21Tr$
Size of US's GDP = 31Tr$

Here is what you can infer:-

1) In China, the wealth can't be easily privatized. The benefits of creation of wealth flows to society.

Therefore, we see inexpensive high quality food, stable rents, good infra.

2) In the US, the wealth is privatized. Entrepreneurs (like Elon) make crazy money. Investors who tag along, can build amazing wealth as well.

3) India: model is being discovered.

Whenever I am having a good year in stock markets, I would take 15-20% profits out.

1) I would go buy something that improves my life.
2) If I am already happy with my current lifestyle, and don't need to "buy" anything -- I would move it to less riskier assets.
3) This process:
- Keeps the joy of investing alive.
- Gives me opportunity to re-invest at lower rates (if market falls).
- If it does not, cool -- the remainder 80% keeps compounding.

Taking profits out is an important part of investing.
Very few folks actually talk about it.

We are witnessing something called as the K-shaped economy almost everywhere in the world.

The rich gets richer.
The poor gets poorer.

However, the difference is: the starting point of wealth.

An average American has a lot of wealth.

If you are the bottom 50%, you might still have a choice to move to regions like South East Asia. And, enjoy a decent quality of life with limited $.

But, if we are in bottom 90% in India, we don't have this option. Our fortunes are tied to economy. We have no option but to hope.

The K-shaped hurts us.
More than we think.

Solution? The bottom 90% rises. Fights for better economic policies.

Better economic policies = more money = wealth = better quality life.

It's that simple.

Question things like job market, Ethanol blending, non-sense taxation, freebies, real returns on stock markets etc.

Anything that can build wealth should be deeply respected. Example: stock markets, FIIs etc. That's a genuine instrument to build wealth for many.

When options to build wealth keeps shrinking, do understand that it's the bottom 90% that gets hit the most.

The top 5-10% always have options.

Ask a simple question: what % of your net worth is in top 100 companies of the world?

For most Indians this answer is 0%.

If you don't consider that to be a problem, you need to go back to the basics of investing.

Going forward, Indian investors are going to track US indices more closely. They will adjust for INR depreciation.

And, then finally calculate their returns.

These things were almost never done. An entire generation of investors were brainwashed by fund managers/podcasters on how: 25K investment created 25 Crores in X years.

That's the only math that ever mattered. Basically a hypothetical retirement in la-la land.

But, the dramatic fall of INR, and the incredible underperformance of the Indian Stock Market has made people more aware of the ground reality.

It has also indicated that there are other (maybe better!) equity markets to invest in.

There are going to be massive changes in how Indians invest (especially in equities) over the next 4-5 years.

The wave is already underway.

Meta makes 1Mn$ profits per employee. And, yet it fired 8000 people.

In 2025: its net income was 79Bn$.
It had 79K employees.

HOWEVER.... it fired 10% of its workforce in 2026.

Not because it "needed" to.
But, because it can. And, it did.

We are basically a cost or a profit centre on a balance sheet.
The sooner we get this, the better our life gets.

We stop attaching emotions to our work.
And, we start prioritizing "us" over a delusional mission of any firm.

Interestingly, that is also how we get rich(er). By investing in ruthless businesses, which lack a soul. But, own humanity.

If you feel conflicted, you have every right to.
But, it doesn't change the reality.

NRIs have become smarter with time.

Earlier when rupee used to fall, they used to send money to India, thinking that: we can buy more rupees with $.

But, now that equation has changed.
They understand that this is a bad move.

Saving in weak currencies is a wealth destruction move.

If rupee constantly keeps falling, in the future they can neither send their kids abroad nor travel abroad for holidays.

- Saving in USD/Euro/SGD
- Keeping your retirement account in US/Europe is a good strategy.
- And, yet living in low cost economies like India/South East Asia.

Allows them to do a "life arbitrage": benefit from strong currency, spend in weak currency.

This idea will get popular with time.

Soon we get to a point where, in order to retire in India, you'd have to invest abroad.

Many are hoping that USD will fall. And, as a result INR will rise. They tag a term called: de-dollarization to justify this.

Well, this is dumb.

De-dollarization does not mean that USD will fall compared to INR.

De-dollarization means: a process where nations, central banks, and global corporations actively reduce their reliance on the USD for international trade, financial transactions, and foreign exchange reserves.

Many countries in the past have tried doing this. For example, Russia and Iran. And, they were cut off from the international economy.

De-dollarization seems great in theory.
But, it is extremely complicated to practically apply.

At one point in time, India started an INR Trade Settlement system, wherein Russia would take INR for supplying oil. After a while, this system was abandoned. Why? Because Russia couldn't do much by holding INR.

USD is the most liquid currency in the world.
Why? Because the US actively pursues the agenda of creating demand for USD.
Right from supplying weapons, debt, tech, and aid.

Any country that goes against the USD pays the price.

The next time you think that USD is going to collapse, think again.

Strength of a currency doesn't come from the free will of different nations. It comes from the brute strength and use-case that aggressor nations create.

1) Best way to learn investing is to invest.
2) Start with 5% of your net-worth. Let's say your net-worth is 1Cr, start with 5L.
3) Use this experiment money to invest safely.
4) First few months: deal with Index.
5) Understand what is resistance, support, good asset, 200DMA.
6) Get a hang of valuation. Why you think Index is under/over-valued? Use ChatGPT/AI to learn.
7) Then do this same exercise on individual good stocks. Like Meta, Msft etc.
8) Bring 5L more capital. Invest across 10 stocks.
9) Just track for 3-4 months. If the stock falls, read why it is falling. If it rises, read the views.
10) Follow contrary opinions on the market. Then form your own opinion.
11) Build spectrum with time. Example: technical analysis, fundamental analysis, etc. Keep learning by investing.

If you invest, you will track.
If you track, you will learn.
If you learn, you will have an alpha.

This is the same philosophy with which I teach on Wisdom Hatch.

And, yes, I write 100% of my content. No AI/Agency :)

USD = 96 Rs today.

Since I wrote this post, 9 months back, INR is down 9.25%.

The core idea remains the same: if your goal is to retire in India, start investing globally.

Travel across South East Asia, and you'd see Americans doing the same. They invest in stable economies/currencies, and live in low-cost regions like SEA.

Where you live and where you retire are two very different things. That's lifestyle arbitrage.

In this decade, this model will get extremely popular.

Trump went to China with his entire business entourage. Why?
My bet is: to set the new world order.

Here are the 3 most obvious things that we are missing. And, it changes our lives.

1) If China <> US gets together, there is no multipolar world. There is this combined superpower versus everyone else.

2) If AI continues to evolve, US and Chinese firms will continue to sell to the entire world. They will get more and more profitable with time.

Most other countries become a short trade, not a long trade.

3) China + US can simply put a line in the world, figure out a way to bifurcate the world, and set a new world order.

You think it won't happen? Maybe. But, it is a possibility that we can't deny.

My trade is US (AI). My plan is to keep 50% of my net-worth in US tech.

If the above macro theory pans out, this 50 units of money goes to 500.

Indian IT services has done an exceptional job in terms of building India.

- It helped build our cities.
- Gave the middle class decent salaries.
- Gave many their first international work experience and onsite opportunities.

It re-trained people on soft and hard skills (something that Indian colleges could never do).

Most importantly, this industry gave people dignity. Walking into a clean-looking office and working alongside educated professionals commanded a certain level of respect.

In simple terms: IT built India's middle class.

We can all complain about working hours, sweatshops, and low salary growth.

But, we need to acknowledge the reality that it gave people homes, cars, and dreams. It helped absorb India's workforce at scale and changed the fortunes of millions.

People who built this industry should be given immense respect.

Think about it: if the IT industry had never taken off, where do you think those people would have worked?

If you're an Indian, here is your best chance to get rich.

Invest in AI.

Logic:-

1) Let's say your job gets disrupted by AI. Who benefits? AI models and AI firms.

You're basically buying insurance.

You lose your job, but your investments in AI help you sustain yourself.

2) If AI fails, cool. You compound your wealth via your existing job. You still win.

3) If your job gets disrupted and you have low or no AI investments, you're running a massive tail-end risk of a wipeout.

AI will either make you richer or make you redundant.
There is no middle path here.

You might be worried about AI valuations. Fair enough.

But, if the AI story compounds over the next 5 years and you miss out, you seriously run a wipeout risk.

No mutual fund manager is going to tell you how to think. I'm trying to share a framework.

Think about this and make better calls.

If your portfolio is big, I am advising you as your elder brother: please set up an investment account outside India. It is painful, but your kids will thank you for this.

There is no end to dealing with:

1) LRS.
2) Constantly thinking about taxes.
3) Worrying about currency depreciation.
4) Not having access to investment options (e.g., selling puts/calls).
5) Not having the freedom to move your capital.

If your portfolio is more than 1.5-2 Cr, start thinking about having global investment accounts. These give you more investment options.

You can buy Singapore REITs, US stocks, European assets, etc. More importantly, you can leverage opportunities.

See, the world is volatile. There are local bubbles bursting every few years. First it was Europe. Right now it seems India. A few years from now, it will be somewhere else.

You will build generational wealth by investing in quality economies during troubled times.

More options = more wealth.

People fire back: 'But since I am residing in India, my tax structure does not change.' True.

Having a global investment account unlocks more investment options. That's the sole purpose. Tax residencies are different.

If you have a good wealth base, start diversifying your options. Your kids will appreciate it :)

How to do this...

You can start by investing in US stocks. This legally gives you a US brokerage account.

And build more options from there.

Dear Readers, if you have kids, invest some money for them in US$ or Euros. This is critical.

You must have observed that the cost of education abroad has become unaffordable now.

Let me speak from my own example. I went to INSEAD in 2014 for my MBA. The total fee was €65,800. Now it is €109,860.

During this time, INR has weakened substantially.

The average EUR/INR rate in 2014 was ₹81.
The current EUR/INR rate is approximately ₹107.

In 2014, the total was 53 Lakhs.
In 2026, this is 1.17 Crores.

Unless somehow we saw an average salary rise of 10% each year, you ain't making it.

For most, this salary rise was 6-7% CAGR in the last 12 years.

Effectively, we are priced out.

*****

INSEAD was just an example to prove how the middle class got priced out of foreign education.

Next comes high-quality asset ownership in India.

Most of the new developments ("supply") in metros are being created exclusively for NRIs. Those shiny villa communities are meant mostly for NRIs. This is their hypothetical home away from home.

You know why this is being done? Because they are a high-quality customer base who can afford to buy these overpriced assets.

You know why? Because they save in $/€.

Want to stay rich? Do what NRIs do (or at least try to).

To retire, you don't need 10 Crores, you need common sense.

FIRE experts are not wrong about the math.
They are wrong about many assumptions.

Here are the top 3:

(1) After retirement, you might NOT want to live in Mumbai, Delhi, or Bengaluru.
Your cost of living in a tier-2 or tier-3 city can come down dramatically.

(2) You might have inheritance. If your parents are financially secure or have an ancestral house, your corpus size requirements shrink significantly.

(3) In this 10 Crore computation, the assumption is that you are growing your wealth at 4% inflation-adjusted returns.

This is abysmally low.
You can do much better.

If you cultivate the ability to 2X your return, you cut your portfolio corpus requirement by half. Does this require work? Yes, of course. Anything worth doing requires effort.

My point is: don't get scared by this 10 Crore number being thrown around. Look at your life situation and make reasonable assumptions.

You might require far less than you think you need.

India is trying to boost its forex reserves. And, if you are an NRI, you could help!

By sending money to India in USD, you can make higher returns and, at the same time, strengthen India's forex reserves.

This is a win-win.

The money NRIs keep in global savings accounts typically earns 2-3% in USD terms. But, if you use the GIFT City structure, you could make up to 6%.
In INR terms, this is 9.5-10% tax-free FD rates.

I am personally using Belong for this and have liked the product, hence recommending it. Check here: https://lnkd.in/dbEBY8Nk

They've made the whole process 100% digital and offer some of the best rates in the market through top-tier partner banks.

#collab

How would you like a 4-hour workday? Well, AI can make that possible.

Right now, people spend roughly 10 hours working 5 days a week.

[8 hours at work, 2 hours in traffic; this is the norm across most major cities]

As AI gets adopted, governments can reform labour laws, institute 4-6 hour workdays, and allow people more time with their families.

I am not sure how we got to the point where working 8 hours a day became the new normal. One of the stupidest things humans have done.

AI saves man-hours. This need not lead to lower headcounts or firing of employees.

Here is how the math could work:

- Before AI: 100 people worked 8-hour shifts.
- After AI: 100 people work 4-hour shifts at lower pay.
- The profits are kept by firms building AI.

Lower salaries, yes. But, you get more free time and less stress.
Many would be okay with this.

Also, AI makes firms more profitable. This means more corporate tax collection for governments. Why not use it to offset the increased cost of living?

INR has fallen 9% in the last 12 months (compared to USD). This is one of the sharpest falls.

Things that will become more expensive:

1) Traveling/vacationing abroad
2) Buying a house (in areas where NRIs buy)
3) Education abroad
4) High fuel prices (we are import-dependent)
5) Flights
6) Freight costs (which will reflect in higher prices)

Unless your income increased by 9%, you got poorer.
That's a fact.

Bash me for saying this: 0.5% official inflation is a joke.

How to save yourself from this?

1) Invest in USD (make at least 30-50% of your portfolio US stocks).
2) Buy hard assets (10-20% in gold, etc.).
3) Buy fixed-supply assets like BTC if you can.
4) Try to earn in USD or more stable currencies.
5) Pray.

When oil prices were $65/barrel, the Indian government sold petrol at ₹90/litre and diesel at ₹85/litre.

They made ₹40/litre.
This divergence helped fund welfare schemes and the fiscal deficit.

Now oil prices have crossed $100/barrel. The prices need to be hiked to more than ₹125/litre.

But, if oil prices rise, they impact everyday cost of living: food, electricity, transportation, rent, etc.

So the government has to make a choice: hike oil prices and impact household bills, or continue with welfare schemes.

What choice do you think the government would make?
